stories.title: Cursor integration for Microsoft Teams
stories.summary: Cursor has released a Microsoft Teams integration that lets users invoke a cloud-based coding agent by mentioning @Cursor in any Teams channel. The agent reads the full thread for context, auto-selects a repository and model based on prompt content and recent agent activity, executes the task in the cloud, and opens a pull request for review — all without leaving Teams. Repository and model selection is heuristic-driven rather than manually specified, inferring intent from conversational history. Teams that previously had to context-switch between chat and IDE to delegate coding tasks can now assign, execute, and review code changes entirely within a Teams channel.
stories.considerations: A concrete scenario: a distributed team's on-call engineer gets a bug report in a Teams channel at 2am, types '@Cursor fix the null-pointer in the payment service based on this stack trace', and wakes up to a PR — no laptop, no IDE session required. The non-obvious implication is that auto-repository selection based on 'recent agent activity' creates a soft coupling between conversation context and codebase targeting, meaning a stale or misread thread could silently route work to the wrong repo — worth auditing PRs carefully until you trust the heuristic. This lands at a moment when async, distributed engineering workflows are the norm and the friction of tool-switching between chat and dev environments is a documented productivity drain. The honest gotcha: 'cloud agent' execution means your code and thread content are being processed server-side by Cursor's infrastructure, which is a non-starter for teams with strict data residency or IP-sensitivity policies — check your org's compliance posture before enabling this broadly.

stories.title: Claude Opus 4.7 fast mode now available
stories.summary: Windsurf has added a 'fast mode' variant of Claude Opus 4.7 to its platform, delivering approximately 2.5x higher output token throughput compared to standard Opus 4.7. The speed increase likely comes from infrastructure-level optimizations — speculative decoding, batching strategies, or dedicated capacity routing — rather than model quantization or capability reduction, since Windsurf explicitly claims full reasoning capabilities are preserved. No context window changes or feature restrictions are mentioned. Developers who were previously throttling their use of Opus 4.7 due to latency in tight edit-compile-test loops can now run it at speeds closer to lighter models like Sonnet.
stories.considerations: In a real agentic coding session — say, refactoring a 50-file TypeScript monorepo where Windsurf is generating, reviewing, and iterating on code in sequence — 2.5x output speed meaningfully compresses wall-clock time per agent step, potentially cutting a 10-minute session to under 5 minutes. The non-obvious implication: faster output at full capability shifts the bottleneck back to the developer's review speed, which may actually expose how much of perceived 'AI slowness' was masking insufficient human review time. This lands at a moment when Sonnet-class models have been eating Opus's lunch on speed-sensitive tasks, so fast mode is a direct response to users defaulting to cheaper/faster models and abandoning Opus entirely. The honest gotcha: 'approximately 2.5x' is a throughput claim, not a latency-to-first-token claim — if TTFT is unchanged, interactive feel may not improve as much as the headline suggests, especially for short completions where streaming speed matters less than initial response delay.

stories.title: gpt-realtime-2 model release
stories.summary: OpenAI released gpt-realtime-2, a new model available across three endpoints: v1/realtime (bidirectional audio interaction), v1/realtime/translations (live audio translation), and v1/realtime/transcription_se (dedicated transcription, likely 'streaming edition'). The model upgrade improves the underlying audio reasoning and response quality compared to the original gpt-realtime-1, though OpenAI has not published specific latency benchmarks or context-window changes in this changelog entry. The dedicated translation and transcription endpoints are new surface area — previously, transcription and translation required separate Whisper API calls outside the realtime session. Developers can now build end-to-end voice pipelines — including multilingual transcription and translation — within a single persistent WebSocket session rather than stitching together multiple APIs.
stories.considerations: A concrete scenario: if you're building a live customer support agent that handles Spanish-speaking users, you can now pipe audio directly into v1/realtime/translations and get translated responses in the same session, eliminating the round-trip latency of a separate Whisper call followed by a Chat Completions call — potentially cutting pipeline latency by hundreds of milliseconds per turn. The non-obvious implication is that dedicated transcription and translation endpoints suggest OpenAI is positioning realtime audio as a first-class modality with its own specialization surface, not just a feature bolted onto the chat API — expect pricing and rate limits to diverge from the main completions tier. This matters now because voice AI applications have been bottlenecked by the awkward multi-API stitching required to get transcription, translation, and response generation in one flow; this consolidates that. The honest gotcha: the changelog gives zero specifics on model quality deltas, latency numbers, or pricing for gpt-realtime-2 versus its predecessor, so before migrating production voice apps you'll need to run your own evals — don't assume 'improved' means improved for your specific accent distribution or domain vocabulary.

stories.title: Filter session history by workspace directory
stories.summary: Continue.dev added workspace-directory-scoped filtering to session history, letting users view chat logs tied to a specific folder rather than a flat global list. The filter works by associating each session with the workspace root active at session creation time, so existing sessions are retroactively bucketed by their originating directory. No new API or config key is required — the filter surfaces in the history UI as a dropdown or toggle scoped to the current open folder. Developers juggling multiple repos in the same Continue installation can now pull up only the sessions relevant to the project they're actively working in.
stories.considerations: If you're context-switching between, say, a monorepo and a separate infrastructure repo daily, this directly cuts the time spent scrolling past irrelevant sessions to find the prompt chain where you debugged that Terraform state issue last Tuesday — previously everything landed in one undifferentiated list. The non-obvious implication: session history is now implicitly tied to how you structure your workspace roots, so developers who open subdirectories ad-hoc (e.g., `code src/` instead of `code .`) may find sessions fragmented across multiple apparent 'workspaces' for the same project. This addresses a real pain point as Continue usage scales — once you have hundreds of sessions across a dozen projects, global history becomes nearly useless for retrieval. Worth noting: sessions created before this release may be attributed to workspace roots based on whatever heuristic Continue uses for backfill, so don't be surprised if older history is miscategorized or lumped into an 'unknown' bucket.

stories.title: Claude Code Hooks: Synchronous Middleware for Every Tool Call
stories.summary: Claude Code Hooks adds synchronous middleware to every tool call in the agentic loop, exposing 25+ named lifecycle points — SessionStart, PreToolUse/PostToolUse pairs for each tool, and Stop — where developers can intercept, block, mutate, or observe Claude's actions before they execute. Each hook is a shell command, HTTP endpoint, or LLM prompt that receives full JSON context on stdin or as a POST body and returns a structured decision (allow/block/modify) that Claude Code enforces. The mechanism is direct injection into the agentic loop itself, not a wrapper or event listener, so there's no race condition between hook execution and tool execution. Exit code 2 is a first-class signal: instead of raising an error, it feeds the hook's stdout back into Claude as context mid-loop — enabling silent, user-invisible corrections. Developers can now enforce org-wide policies (no writes outside /src, no curl to external hosts), validate schema before file commits, or inject runtime context into Claude's reasoning without modifying Claude Code's internals.
stories.considerations: In a 300-file TypeScript monorepo, a PreToolUse hook on Write/Edit can run tsc --noEmit against the proposed file content before it lands on disk — catching type errors that would otherwise silently accumulate across a long agentic session and require a full rollback. The exit code 2 behavior is the non-obvious gem here: a hook can detect that Claude is about to use a deprecated internal API, inject the correct replacement into context, and let Claude self-correct — all without surfacing a disruptive error or interrupting the user's flow, which is a qualitatively different capability than just blocking bad actions. This lands at exactly the right moment: as agentic coding sessions get longer and touch more files, the blast radius of a single bad tool call grows, and teams need deterministic guardrails that don't rely on prompt engineering or hoping Claude reads the CLAUDE.md correctly. The honest gotcha: hooks run synchronously and block the loop, so a slow HTTP endpoint or a hung shell command will stall the entire session — there's no timeout configuration mentioned in the docs, which means a flaky hook in a CI environment could deadlock a long-running agent with no graceful recovery path.
